speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1995. The survivor is David Jakubowski, J-A-K-U-B-O-S-K-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, W-O-S-K--

speaker: interviewer
text: K-O-W--

speaker: interviewee
text: K-U--

speaker: interviewee
text: --B-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, thank you. And this is tape number 4.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I ended-- we ended the last tape when I said we were on the street because we had to leave the burning house. There were thousands of people standing in the middle of the street. And every three yards or four yards was a machine gun-- a heavy machine gun with two soldiers. And I told you the story about the General Jurgen von Stroop. So from there, from the street they took us to the Umschlagplatz to the huge building there, in which I had the ambulatory. But now I was in it to be, as they called, 'resettled.' Again, and we were only a few hours. But it was horrible because, you know, the, the, the Ukrainian, Latvian, Estonian, and Lithuanian police treated us horrible. They were kicking us, beating, you know, screaming. But after a few hours, they took us out to the trains. And they pushed, as I said before, 200, yeah, 200 people into one cattle car. It was even impossible to turn around. They closed the door. And we stayed there in, in, in-- on the Warsaw-- in Warsaw in the trains for a few hours until they started rolling in the evening-- late evening. Now, the whole night, a lot of people were dying there. We were thirsty. I have to say something because what-- about the Poles. We were so thirsty. And the Poles-- when the train stopped several times, some of the farmers and Polish guys were coming close. And one-- I remember one lady from the ghetto. She was very rich. She was screaming, bring us water, we'll pay you! Bring us water, in Polish. So he said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we'll bring you water. He came back with a bottle. She threw him out a 20, 20 coin-- 20, 20 golden dollars. And he gave her the bottle of water. It was urine. This was just to tell you how-- what they thought about us. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, around 5:30, 6 o'clock, we arrived at a regular station. We were sure that we were going to Treblinka, just like any other person-- like any other transport. We came, and they opened the door. It was a railroad station.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the first thing, the German soldiers gave us water to drink. You have any idea what is water, how water tastes when you're dying from thirst? I myself probably drank half a bucket. And everybody else did the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we left-- from 200 people, only 60-- 50 or 60 survived. The rest were dead. And when we came out, there were soldiers, Polish soldiers, who were working-- helping the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: We asked them, where are we? They said, you are in Lublin, Station Lublin. And you are going all to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we knew what Majdanek was. This was a killer camp. I mean, first of all, they treated there the people worse than animals. They worked them to death. And then they gassed them and burned them. The commanding officer in Lublin was Goth-- Goeth-- G-O-T-Z-E, Gotze. The Polish government caught him after the war and then hanged him. They hanged him. And we were in rows of 12-- old men, young men, children, woman, mixed-- in front of a huge gate. And I was in the fourth or fifth row from the left side now. And a yard, a couple of yards from us was a SS major standing there, giving orders to open the doors, to start to leave to, to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly, there appears a, a German Oberscharfuhrer, a sergeant. And he says to the major, Major-- no-- Obersturmbannfuhrer, I have to have 300 mens. He started screaming, are you crazy? Those people are ready to go to Majdanek. And you want me to take out 300 men now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I have to have them. They are for the German machine war. They you have to work in the Heinkel-- in the Heinkel producing planes. He said, all right! Take them, the 300. But you have four to five minutes time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was running from one row to another, grabbed here at the jackets. And raus, he would scream, out! And it took him probably four or five minutes and moved us all to a different square on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people who were standing, who left there, they all went to Majdanek. There was-- Nachum Remba, too-- not one survived. We came to the other square. And around us--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were one chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chosen. Now, we were 300 people-- 300 men. And the first order-- sit down on the ground! And around us there were the Blacks-- the Ukrainian police-- and the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first question of the commander officer, Oberscharfuhrer, was, are there any physicians here? We stood up. There were seven of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, hinsetzen. Sit down. They put us again into trains, 200 in one wagon. But this was only a short ride, 45 minutes. And we came to a labor camp, Budzyn-- B-U-D-Z-Y-N.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came there, again, he called, physicians, raus! So we came out. And he asked them, what your speciality? What your speciality?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took two physicians. He put them in the ambulatory, in the camp ambulatory-- this was a working camp, labor camp-- and four physicians in the hospital. There was a hospital. Me, he says-- I was general physician. So he said, you're the only one. You go with me to the factory. You'll be-- you'll be the factory physician-- the camp there, because the Jews were working there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, later on, I found out-- we found out that he was a barber in civilian life. And before the war, you know, the barbers have a certain respect for the physician because they were doing some, some work, too. That's why his first question was, who are the doctors? So all the physicians were-- all the men were registered and sent to different barracks. There were seven huge barracks, six for men and one for women. The physicians got a smaller room. We got a smaller room and-- for ourselves. They were-- for the-- for the seven of us. The rest of were put in the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he was a killer, that Oberscharfuhrer. Feigs was his name, F-E-I-G-S. They caught him after the war. I don't know what happened to him. And every morning, a group, few hundred people, were walking every morning out to the factory. He was in the front on a horse, you know, and orders, sing! Everybody was singing. And he let me walk on the side with my little bag. Now, this was a camp, as I said, where they were working-- where they were preparing parts for, for Heinkel fighter planes. There were a lot of Poles working there, too, under German supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: Civilians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Civilians, yeah. Now, we could buy, who had money-- I had the ambulatory there. So I had plenty of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plenty of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plenty of food. But you could buy food from the Poles. If you had any money, you could buy food, food from them. They were selling everything. The work wasn't too hard, you know. We were in contact with the Poles, you know. It wasn't-- this was a labor camp only. But in the camp, this was horror there because the daily supply of food, the rations were very meager. They were dying from starvation there, unless somebody had money and could buy through one of the men who were going to the factory to buy some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, I had a little fellow who was working with me. And he was so smart. He was bringing me soups and everything--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a--

speaker: interviewee
text: --whatever I needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jew-- a Jewish boy. He was 14, 15 years old. Oh--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he get--

speaker: interviewee
text: --brilliant.

speaker: interviewer
text: --get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was an organizer, you know. You had to be smart in the camp, too, a little bit. He was an organizer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was working there. And several-- two very unpleasant-- one very unpleasant thing happened to me. I was-- I had an ambulatory in the other building. I had that one ambulatory for the Jews in the Jewish part.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had another ambulatory for-- a big ambulatory together with the Polish guy. He was not a physician. He was a student, second or third year of medicine. One morning when I was sitting there, there were two barbers there, Jews, who were shaving and cutting hair, the Germans, too. And they had a room next to my ambulatory. So one morning, he comes in, one of them says, Doctor, come to me. Nobody of the Germans is coming in. This morning I give you a shave.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in. And my luck, a German come in-- came in. He started screaming. He said, a Jew, where only the Germans can come in? So he says, this is a doctor. I use a razor blade that I use only on the Jews. He says, doesn't matter. A Jew here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me to a part of the factory. And as punishment, I was hit 25 times with an iron stick at my buttocks. I couldn't sleep on my-- I slept on my belly for two weeks until it healed. This was the time where, in the factory, a lot of German were patient of mine because he was a student. All the fractures, dislocations, any disease, they were coming to me. Even the director of the factory was a patient of mine. But it doesn't matter. I was a Jew. In another comic situation, one day, I was in the ambulatory. It comes in an SS man, 6' 5', 6' 6', so tall and skinny. I mean, the, the room, the, the ceiling almost [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're the doctor? I said, yes. All right, treat me. He had a horrible abscess on the back. I cleaned it out. I took the pus out-- cleaned from the pus. And I put a dressing, you know. And he felt-- I could see it, that he felt much better.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, he looked at me. And he took out a pack of cigarettes and took once one cigarette out and threw it on the floor. So I, I picked it up, and I gave it to him. He says, keep it. So for all what I did for him-- but I was a Jew. And he was a Superman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were working, and every day in the evening, were coming to the-- again, the same way, to the camp. And there was an Appell roll. You know what an Appell roll.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a huge square. And every barrack stood, stood separate. Now, the Appell roll is unforgettable-- the screaming and beatings. The Ukrainian and the SS men, when they found somebody or something not in a straight line, the SS were hitting in the groin. And the Ukrainians beating, so after-- and him on the horse. And after every Appell there were always a few on the ground wounded. And we had to take them to the-- to the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One night-- there was a huge camp with wires, you know, with wires-- barbed wires. One evening, the latrine-- you know what latrine was? It was close to the wires on the other side of the barracks. Four Jews run, run, I mean, they run away. They cut the wires and run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now hell broke out. They started shooting. Whoever they found in the latrine was shot right away. And he run-- Oberscharfuhrer Feigs run into my barrack. And I was still up. And he started screaming and beating and screaming. And you, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] because-- he called us 'Banditen.' Because of you, they ran away! You helped them to run away! And he was beating me. And then a Ukrainian came and started to beat me with his rifle that I think-- I thought-- you know, rrrrup, pulling my hair out-- I thought that I'm going to be killed. But I noticed that one of the other officers of the SS said something to him into his ear. And he says, leave him alone. I survived again. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of that young boy that helped you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Pintele' we called him. I don't-- that's the first name. Oh, Helen remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: His first name was Pintele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pintele-- and 12, 13. He was smart. He was helpful. He was bringing everything. He was cooking soups for me-- unbelievable. That Pintele was wearing black eyes, beautiful. I don't know if he even survived, Pintele. I'll never forget this boy in my life, Pintele.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-I-N-T-E-L-E, Pintele. What a boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I said, in the camp, the life was one horror because they, they didn't have anything to eat. You were dying. But who were bringing food and who had money had something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that labor camp in early fall, they brought a group of people from Hrubieszow. It's a-- it was near Lublin. That's where she came from. That's where I met her-- my wife, my future wife. And they were working in the camp, too. In early winter-- it was a horrible winter, very cold. Early winter, they changed that labor camp to a concentration camp. It was very cold. Everybody was starving, you know. And it was horrible. I forgot to mention one thing. The, the, the camp was run internally by POWs, Polish-- but they were all Jews, but POWs. And the commanding officer was a former major in the Polish army, a Jew, but a gentleman. Stockman was his name. I can't forget certain names-- a gentleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stockman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, S-T-O-C-K-M-A-N. So when they changed into-- of concentration camps, it was horrible. There was nothing to eat. We got first-- we got to blue-and-white stripes uniforms, which were as thin as paper. And it was cold, frost, below zero centigrade-- horrible. And they still were working. Bu they were-- they couldn't-- there was no contact with Poles any more. We were mostly in the concentration camps. Some were working outside in the factory. But it was not the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: This occurred when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in February 1944. And a camp in Mielec asked for a physician. They had to-- they had to have another physician. I volunteered to be-- I was the first one to volunteer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sent me there with a group of men. And this was still a labor camp. And they were working with there, with the Poles, too. They were working for the Heinkel Werke.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the work wasn't difficult there. It was much easier. I was working as a physician. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They didn't starve there. There was enough food. The kitchen was run by Jews, you know. There was enough food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there-- then they changed this to a concentration camp again. And the concentration camp crew, these orders came from Plaszow. It was a concentration camp in Krakau. Oh, the, the commanding officer was Schwamberger. I have his picture. I will show you. Schwamberger, he was a killer. He was killing right and left, right and left. It was horrible. Now, the Jews were working in the factory there together with the Poles. Now, when they were leaving-- the Poles-- the factory, we were coming in the evening to the Appell. The Poles were leaving the factory. And they had-- and one day, a Jew was among them. Now, when they found out, they tattooed our wrists with a K-L, 'Konzentrationslager,' in German. And everyone who was leaving the factory had to show their wrist.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you notice that my K-L is very small. Normally, they tattooed this around 6 to 8 inches, like this. Now, why is mine so small?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the soldier who was tattooing everybody was a patient of mine. I treated him for what they call it now, sexually transmitted disease. Now in my time, it was called gonorrhea. So I, I cured him. And he was so thankful, when he was doing this, he said to me in German-- but I'll say it in English-- Herr Doctor-- he even gave me the title-- I will make you a small and pretty K-L. He did me a great a favor. [LAUGHS] It's-- these are moments which are unforgettable, you know, in somebody's life. Now, in Mielec, at a certain time when there was a concentration camp, the work stopped because the Russians were approaching. So they closed the factory. And they closed the camp and sent everyone out to Wieliczka. This is a small village in Poland, where they were, for hundreds of years, salt mines-- and deep, thousands of miles down. I went down there. They had a factory-- Heinkel factory in planes. They were producing planes there. But it was really something-- but there were so many of us, they didn't need us. So they sent a big group to Flossenburg. Flossenburg was a camp in southern Germany, in south Germany-- a killer camp, 35,000 to 40,000 people there. Now, when I came there, we were working in the stone quarries, cutting stones and carrying them from one place to another. Two-- I was working with another guy from here to there, and from there back to here. That's nothing. That's a whole day. And one soldier, SS soldier with sub-machine gun, screaming, schnell! Fast, fast, faster! Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ration was food, so we're getting around 250 calories a day. So every three, four, four weeks, five weeks maximum, they had to change-- the, the new transports were coming in. And I was working. And I felt I that I'm dying. And one day I was standing and working there. And I saw suddenly a man in striped clothes with the SS soldier. And I recognized the guy. He was a doctor who was working with me in Warsaw in the Czyste hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I called him, Dr. Goldberg-- I remembered even his name-- Dr. Goldberg. He looked at me. He says-- [NON-ENGLISH], they called me. [NON-ENGLISH], what are you doing here? And I said, you see what I am doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he was the chief doctor of the camp for the inmates. And he was an excellent physician. So some German were his patients, too, because they had a lot of sexually-transmitted diseases there, because there was a woman there for the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he took right out. He had the right. Took me out. And he put me in a barrack as a barrack physician. Now, there I recovered fast because I had more food and I didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the worst thing in there, besides the work, was the Appells-- Appell rolls. I remember one. It was cold-- winter in February. It was probably 30, 40 degrees below centigrade. And next to me were standing on one side, on the right side, an elderly man. He was a professor of-- professor of English at the Sorbonne University. And on the other side was a-- no, Latin, professor of Latin. And on the other side of me was a mayor from a town near Paris. They dropped dead from the cold because we were in the same clothes. And they were not, not fed well. They were good friends of mine. I miss them. But there is one thing else what happened there, which is unforgettable. It was written even up in, in books. One morning during the morning Appell, one was missing. On 35,000, one was missing. Now, the SS soldiers, they went completely wild, beating and screaming. They couldn't find that one guy. So they said, perhaps they miscounted the dead ones. Because every day, 30, 40, 50 people were dying in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put all the corpses out on a-- in a-- in a-- in a-- square there. Over a thousand in three-- over thousand of them, we saw it. And it was-- it is known because it was written up in the book, Over a thousand-- and the guy was missing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a young boy who overslept. He was in one of the barracks. And they found him. He overslept. And he came out. And they shot him right away. Now, as I said, they had to-- hundreds were coming every few weeks, new. And new guys were coming to the camp because they were dying out. And I was in the camp. And I, I was not doing-- I was in the barrack, not doing too, too bad. And it came fall of 1944. And one day, Dr. Goldberg, the chief doctor, comes to me and said, David, you are my best friend in this camp. I don't have anybody else. Americans are approaching. We heard even artillery fire. And we were digging, you know, against the tanks-- holes against tanks, something. I don't know what you called it, outside-- the work. He said, he came to me, he said, David, you're the only friend I have here. I'm going to send you to Hersbruck to organize a hospital there. Because here, when the-- before the Americans came, over 35,000-- the whole camp-- will be finished. And they did kill them all off. In the beginning of January, I went with a group, a few hundred, to Hersbruck. Now, we came to the camp there. There was a camp before it. It was SS. SS lived there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a huge building. And I organized a hospital for 50 beds. And I got a room for myself. Can you imagine that's what it meant, a room-- for myself, a room?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got very sick there, typhus. But I was by myself. So somehow-- after, after two weeks, they sent another physician in, a kapo for the whole hospital. But this was Ukrainian. He was the best friend of mine from studies from Krakau. We studied together through the exams. So I was running the internal medicine department. And he was running the diarrhea department. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I was sick, I mean, everything was all right. And one day in January, a couple of weeks after we came, a few of the inmates, Jews, came to me and said, listen, Doctor, the chief Orthodox rabbi from Warsaw, Rabbi David Shapiro, is dying from exhaustion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because you couldn't-- you couldn't live longer than two weeks there. They was working so hard in the mine-- in the-- in the mountains, hills, you know, factories, beautiful, excellent factories. So this was Rabbi David Shapiro. And I took him in to my-- to the hospital. It just happened that I knew the guy from Warsaw. He was living in the same building that I did. And then, he came from the Warsaw. He went to the same camp in Budzyn. But he was two years there the rabbi. And we, you know, we know each other-- we knew each other very well. I learned-- he was with other rabbi. And I learned from him Jewish. So--
